what_it_does: Discovers a running tts-* service from a local registry, POSTs text to it, and writes the returned WAV audio to .data/voice; it does not play audio.
observations: Reads ops/services.json to find services whose keys start with tts- and have a port.
Synthesizes speech by POSTing JSON {"text": ..., optional "voice": ...} to 127.0.0.1:<port>/.
Validates the response starts with the RIFF WAV magic bytes.
Writes output to REPO/.data/voice with a deterministic-ish name based on the current count of .wav files.
Uses only the standard library (urllib, json, os, time).
Public API: engines(), running_engine(), speak(); playback is explicitly left to the caller.
standards: Error messages are 'TEACHING-loud' and tell the operator how to bring an engine online.
RIFF magic-byte check is used to detect JSON error responses masquerading as audio.
Filename is derived from existing .wav count rather than timestamp or randomness.
conventions: Docstrings explain behavior and design intent.
Paths are resolved relative to the repository root.
concerns: Deterministic filename from file count is not concurrency-safe and can collide if files are removed.
services.json is opened with json.load(open(...)) and not explicitly closed.
running_engine() re-checks health for each service, creating a small race window.
Only the first live engine is used; there is no fallback or selection policy.
No retry on transient network errors.
notes: A thin, registry-aware TTS broker that turns text into a local WAV by delegating to whichever tts-* engine is currently up.
questions: How is ops/services.json kept in sync with the actual running TTS ports?
What voice identifiers are valid for each engine, and where are they documented?
Is the file-count-based filename scheme safe under concurrent synthesis calls?
Should non-RIFF audio formats be supported in the future?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it surfaces a running TTS engine, synthesizes text to a WAV, and leaves playback to the device side.
apparent_themes: Local service discovery from a registry.
Thin adapter over external TTS engines.
intention_signals: Provide a simple, engine-agnostic runtime API for turning text into speech by routing to whichever TTS service is currently alive, while keeping the fabric uninvolved in playback.
novelty: The 'TEACHING-loud' error style and RIFF-magic validation to distinguish audio from JSON error responses; deterministic WAV numbering from existing file count.
contribution: A lightweight, self-contained TTS client that discovers engines via a local registry, health-checks them, synthesizes speech through the first available engine, validates the returned audio, and persists a WAV file for downstream playback.
summary_for_embedding: cc_voice.py is a local Python runtime module that discovers available tts-* services from a company registry, health-checks them, posts text to the first live engine, and writes the returned WAV to .data/voice.
intention_for_embedding: Intended to give text a voice by surfacing a running TTS engine without reimplementing synthesis, leaving playback to the operator or UI.

symbols.does: RuntimeError subclass for loud, instructive TTS failures.
symbols.description: Raised when no TTS engine is available or synthesis fails; its docstring and messages emphasize being 'TEACHING-loud' rather than silent.

symbols.does: Parses the local registry to enumerate tts-* services.
symbols.description: Loads ops/services.json and returns a list of (key, port, title) tuples for every registered service whose key starts with tts- and has a port.

symbols.does: Probes whether a localhost service port responds with HTTP 200 on /health.
symbols.description: Performs a short HTTP GET to 127.0.0.1:<port>/health with a 3-second timeout and returns True only for status 200.

symbols.does: Returns all registered TTS engines and marks the first one that is up.
symbols.description: Builds a list of engine metadata including health status and reports the first live engine under the 'up' key.

symbols.does: Returns the first live tts-* engine or raises a teaching VoiceError.
symbols.description: Iterates registered tts-* services, returns the (key, port) of the first one whose /health succeeds, and otherwise raises VoiceError with guidance on starting an engine.

symbols.does: Sends text to the running TTS engine and saves the returned WAV.
symbols.description: Validates the input text, picks the running engine, POSTs JSON to its root endpoint, checks that the response begins with RIFF, writes the audio to .data/voice, and returns metadata including engine, path, byte size, and a sample-format hint.
